issues.suggestion: Consider simplifying this function
Consider breaking this function into smaller functions

issues.message: Empty type alias: IUpdate = {} — missing properties

issues.description: Password comparison uses hashing instead of bcrypt.compare(). The code hashes the provided current_password and compares the hash strings directly (`currentPasswordHash !== member.password_hash`). This is incorrect for bcrypt because bcrypt generates different salts each time, so two hashes of the same password will never be equal. This means password verification will always fail, effectively locking users out of password changes. Additionally, if PasswordUtil.hash() uses a deterministic algorithm (non-bcrypt), it would be a cryptographic weakness.
issues.suggestion: Use PasswordUtil.verify(props.body.current_password, member.password_hash) instead of hashing and comparing. The verify function should use bcrypt.compare() internally, which handles salt comparison correctly.

issues.description: The todoId path parameter in the controller (MultiusertodoMemberTodosController.ts, update method, line ~175) is typed as `string` without the `tags.Format<'uuid'>` constraint, unlike the `at` and `erase` methods which also use plain `string`. The provider function signature declares `todoId: string & tags.Format<'uuid'>` but the controller passes an unvalidated string. This type mismatch means the UUID format validation at the provider level is a TypeScript compile-time check only and may not be enforced at runtime, potentially allowing malformed IDs to reach the database query.
issues.suggestion: Change the `@TypedParam('todoId') todoId: string` in the `update` and `erase` methods of MultiusertodoMemberTodosController to `todoId: string & tags.Format<'uuid'>` to ensure runtime UUID validation via nestia/typia.

issues.description: The member refresh token endpoint looks up sessions by the raw refresh_token string value from the database without first verifying the JWT signature. While the token is a JWT, the code skips jwt.verify() and directly queries the database using the token as a lookup key. This means a forged or tampered token string could potentially match a database record if an attacker can predict or enumerate token values. The guest refresh (postMultiUserTodoAuthGuestRefresh.ts) correctly verifies the JWT signature first.
issues.suggestion: Before querying the database, verify the JWT signature using jwt.verify(props.body.refreshToken, MyGlobal.env.JWT_SECRET_KEY) and extract the session_id from the decoded payload. Then query the database by session_id rather than by the raw token string, similar to the guest refresh implementation.

issues.description: The join endpoint accepts an `ip` field in the request body (`props.body.ip ?? props.ip`) allowing clients to override the server-detected IP address. This undermines IP-based security auditing and session tracking, as malicious users can spoof any IP address in their session record.
issues.suggestion: Remove the `ip` field from the IJoin request body interface and always use the server-detected IP (`props.ip` from NestJS `@Ip()` decorator). Client-supplied IP values should never be trusted for security-sensitive fields.

issues.description: The guest refresh token rotation is incomplete. While a new refresh token is generated, the session record in the database is only updated with the new `expired_at` value but the new refresh token is NOT stored in the session record. The old refresh token remains valid in the database, allowing replay attacks where a stolen old refresh token can still be used to generate new access tokens.
issues.suggestion: Update the session record to store the new refresh token: add `refresh_token: refreshToken` (and optionally `access_token: accessToken`) to the data object in the `multi_user_todo_guest_sessions.update()` call.

issues.description: The GET /:sessionId endpoint returns full session details including accessToken and refreshToken according to the specification comment ('Return all session fields including id, memberId, accessToken, refreshToken'). Exposing active tokens via an API endpoint creates a significant risk — if an attacker gains read access to session data (e.g., via IDOR or logging), they can hijack active sessions.
issues.suggestion: Never return full access_token or refresh_token values in API responses. Return only masked/truncated token prefixes for identification purposes. The specification comment itself notes 'Do NOT return full access_token or refresh_token values' for the index endpoint — apply the same rule to the detail endpoint.

issues.description: Authentication failures (invalid credentials, expired tokens) are not logged with sufficient context. The controllers use console.log(error) for generic error handling, but failed login attempts, token refresh failures, and password change attempts should be logged with IP address and timestamp for security monitoring and intrusion detection.
issues.suggestion: Implement structured security event logging for authentication failures, including IP address, timestamp, and failure reason. Consider integrating with a centralized logging system.

issues.description: No rate limiting is implemented on authentication endpoints (login, join, refresh). This allows brute-force attacks against passwords and token enumeration. The specification comment mentions 'Rate limit 5 attempts per IP per minute' but this is not implemented in the code.
issues.suggestion: Implement rate limiting using a NestJS guard or middleware (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) on the login, join, and refresh endpoints. Apply stricter limits to login (e.g., 5 attempts/minute/IP) than to other endpoints.

issues.description: Password complexity validation beyond minimum length is not enforced. The specification mentions 'minimum 8 characters' but no complexity requirements (uppercase, numbers, special characters) are validated.
issues.suggestion: Add password complexity validation in the IJoin and IUpdatePassword type definitions using typia tags, or implement validation in the provider layer using a password strength library.

issues.description: The session query filters by `multi_user_todo_member_id: props.guest.id`, but the field name suggests this is a member session table being queried with a guest ID. If the table stores member sessions, a guest should not be able to access member session data. This may be a semantic mismatch that allows cross-role data access.
issues.suggestion: Verify that `multi_user_todo_member_sessions` is the correct table for guest session queries. If guests have their own session table (`multi_user_todo_guest_sessions`), use that instead. Ensure the ownership filter correctly scopes data to the authenticated guest.

issues.description: The session is fetched by `findUniqueOrThrow` without an ownership filter in the query itself. The ownership check is done post-fetch by comparing `session.member.id !== props.guest.id`. This means the DB always fetches the record regardless of ownership, and the check relies on the `member` relation being correctly populated. If the transformer or select omits the member relation, the check could silently fail or throw a runtime error instead of a 403.
issues.suggestion: Move the ownership filter into the Prisma query itself: `where: { id: props.sessionId, multi_user_todo_guest_id: props.guest.id }` to enforce authorization at the database level and avoid post-fetch ownership checks.

issues.description: The JWT verification does not explicitly specify the `algorithms` option. Without restricting the allowed algorithms, the `none` algorithm attack or algorithm confusion (e.g., RS256 vs HS256) could be exploited depending on the jsonwebtoken version and configuration.
issues.suggestion: Pass `{ algorithms: ['HS256'] }` (or whichever algorithm is used for signing) as the third argument to `jwt.verify()` to explicitly restrict accepted algorithms: `jwt.verify(token, MyGlobal.env.JWT_SECRET_KEY, { algorithms: ['HS256'] })`.

issues.description: The guest join endpoint accepts a client-supplied `ip` field (`props.body.ip ?? props.ip`) and stores it in the session. A client can spoof their IP address by providing a fake value in the request body, undermining any IP-based session tracking or security controls.
issues.suggestion: Always use the server-determined IP (`props.ip`) for session storage. Do not trust client-supplied IP values. Remove `props.body.ip` from the session creation data.

issues.description: JWT verification failures are silently swallowed in the catch block with no logging. Failed authentication attempts are not recorded, making it impossible to detect brute-force or token-stuffing attacks.
issues.suggestion: Add structured logging for authentication failures, including the timestamp and (non-sensitive) request metadata, to support security monitoring and incident response.

issues.description: The `limit` parameter for pagination is user-controlled with a default of 20 but no maximum cap enforced in this provider. A client could request an arbitrarily large number of records, causing excessive database load.
issues.suggestion: Enforce a maximum limit (e.g., `const limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 20, 100)`) to prevent resource exhaustion from large page size requests.

issues.description: The default `limit` is set to 100, which is unusually high and user-controlled with no enforced maximum. This could lead to large data dumps in a single request.
issues.suggestion: Apply a reasonable maximum cap on the limit parameter, e.g., `const limit = Math.min(props.body.limit ?? 20, 100)`.

agentEvaluations.summary: [2 chunks, 17→16 issues] The codebase implements a multi-user todo application with reasonable access control patterns (MemberAuth/GuestAuth decorators, ownership verification in queries). However, there are critical issues: (1) Password verification in the password change flow is fundamentally broken due to incorrect use of hash comparison instead of bcrypt.compare, effectively preventing password changes; (2) UUID validation is missing on todoId path parameters for update/delete operations; (3) Member refresh tokens are not verified cryptographically before database lookup, unlike the more secure guest refresh implementation; (4) Guest refresh token rotation is incomplete — new tokens are generated but not persisted, allowing replay attacks. Additionally, client-supplied IP override in the join endpoint undermines audit integrity, and session detail endpoints may expose full token values. The authentication architecture is generally sound with JWT-based sessions and token rotation concepts, but the implementation has several security gaps that need addressing.

issues.description: Importing non-existent module '@prisma/sdk'. Prisma client is typically imported from '@prisma/client', not '@prisma/sdk'.
issues.suggestion: Change import to: import { Prisma } from '@prisma/client';

issues.description: Logic error: When todo is marked as complete, the code fetches existing completed_at from database, but this query doesn't include completed_at in the initial select. The code tries to access completed_at from a query that only selected id, title, description, start_date, due_date, is_complete, deleted_at.
issues.suggestion: Add 'completed_at: true' to the initial select clause, or calculate completed_at based on current state without additional query.

issues.description: Logic error: The additional query to fetch completed_at uses findUnique with only completed_at selected, but this will return null if the todo doesn't exist (though it should). More importantly, the logic is flawed - if todo is already complete, it should preserve existing completed_at, not fetch it again.
issues.suggestion: Include completed_at in the initial select and use that value directly instead of making a second query.

issues.description: Incomplete implementation: The profile update function only updates 'updated_at' timestamp but doesn't actually update any profile fields (like display name mentioned in requirements).
issues.suggestion: Add logic to update actual profile fields from props.body (e.g., display name).

issues.description: Logic error: Password verification compares hashed current password with stored hash, but should compare plaintext current password with stored hash using PasswordUtil.verify.
issues.suggestion: Change to: const isValid = await PasswordUtil.verify(props.body.current_password, member.password_hash); if (!isValid) throw...

issues.description: Logic error: Session expiration check compares ISO strings instead of Date objects, which may fail due to string formatting differences.
issues.suggestion: Compare Date objects directly: if (session.expired_at <= new Date())

issues.description: Logic error: Sets expired_at to refreshExpires (7 days) but access token expires in 15 minutes. Should store access token expiration separately or use refresh token expiration for session.
issues.suggestion: Consider storing both access and refresh expiration times, or clarify session expiration policy.

issues.description: Logic error: Uses toISOStringSafe to create Date objects (new Date(createdAt)), but toISOStringSafe returns string. Creating Date from ISO string is redundant.
issues.suggestion: Use Date objects directly: created_at: new Date(), updated_at: new Date()

issues.description: Logic error: Type assertion 'createdAt satisfies string as string' is incorrect syntax and unnecessary.
issues.suggestion: Remove the type assertion or fix syntax: created_at: new Date(createdAt),

issues.description: Requirements mismatch: Function name suggests it operates on guest sessions, but it queries member_sessions table and uses member ID filter. Guest should not have access to member sessions.
issues.suggestion: Either rename function to handle member sessions, or change implementation to query guest_sessions table.

issues.description: Logic error: Sets deleted_at to ISO string but database column expects DateTime. Should use new Date() instead of new Date().toISOString().
issues.suggestion: Change to: deleted_at: new Date()

issues.description: Unused imports across multiple files: IEntity, ArrayUtil, jwt, typia, tags, v4, PasswordUtil, toISOStringSafe are imported but not used in many provider files.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports from each file to clean up code.

issues.description: Inconsistent naming: Function parameter 'request' but calls jwtAuthorize with object { request: request }.
issues.suggestion: Align parameter naming or call directly: jwtAuthorize({ request })

agentEvaluations.summary: Code has critical hallucination issues with '@prisma/sdk' imports across all provider files. Several logic errors in todo completion, password verification, and date handling. Profile update is incomplete. Some requirements mismatches with guest/member session access. Overall structure follows AutoBE conventions but needs significant fixes.

issues.description: Controller implements guest session endpoints that have no basis in the Prisma schema. The schema defines guest sessions (multi_user_todo_guest_sessions) but there is no OpenAPI specification provided for guest session operations. The controller implements PATCH /multiUserTodo/guest/sessions and GET /multiUserTodo/guest/sessions/:sessionId which are completely fabricated routes with no spec mapping.
issues.suggestion: Remove this controller entirely unless there is an OpenAPI specification defining guest session operations.

issues.description: Controller uses wrong DTO types (IMultiUserTodoMemberSession) for guest session operations. Guest sessions should use guest-specific DTOs, not member session DTOs. This creates a type mismatch between the actor type (guest) and the data structure (member session).
issues.suggestion: Use appropriate guest session DTOs if they exist, or create proper guest session structures.

issues.description: Controller implements DELETE /multiUserTodo/member/sessions endpoint that has no basis in the provided specification. No OpenAPI spec is provided for member session management operations.
issues.suggestion: Remove this controller unless there is an OpenAPI specification defining member session termination.

issues.description: Controller implements history endpoints but the spec comment mentions rejecting access if todo.deleted_at is not null, which contradicts the business requirement that history should be accessible for todos in trash. The Prisma schema shows cascade delete only on permanent deletion, not soft deletion.
issues.suggestion: Remove the restriction on accessing history for soft-deleted todos, as history should be preserved and accessible even when todos are in trash.

issues.description: Provider imports unnecessary dependencies (jwt, PasswordUtil, ArrayUtil) that are not used in the implementation. This adds irrelevant code that has no basis in the specification.
issues.suggestion: Remove unused imports to clean up the code.

issues.description: Provider name 'patchMultiUserTodoGuestSessions' suggests guest sessions, but implementation queries 'multi_user_todo_member_sessions' table (member sessions). This is a fundamental mismatch between the intended entity type and the actual database model being accessed.
issues.suggestion: Either rename the provider to match member sessions (e.g., 'patchMultiUserTodoMemberSessions') or change the implementation to query 'multi_user_todo_guest_sessions' table if guest sessions are intended.

issues.description: Setting 'deleted_at' to a string value (new Date().toISOString()) instead of a Date object. Prisma schema defines 'deleted_at' as DateTime? which expects Date objects, not strings.
issues.suggestion: Change to 'new Date()' instead of 'new Date().toISOString()'.

issues.description: Setting 'created_at' and 'updated_at' fields with string values (createdAt) instead of Date objects. Prisma schema defines these as DateTime fields which expect Date objects.
issues.suggestion: Pass Date objects instead of strings: 'created_at: new Date(createdAt)' and 'updated_at: new Date(createdAt)'.

issues.description: Implementation updates 'updated_at' timestamp but doesn't actually update any member profile fields from the request body. The function accepts 'IMultiUserTodoMember.IUpdate' but doesn't use it to modify member data.
issues.suggestion: Either implement actual profile field updates using the request body, or rename/refactor the function to match its actual behavior.

agentEvaluations.summary: [2 chunks, 14→9 issues] Significant hallucinations detected: 7 phantom endpoints without spec basis, 2 spec mismatches, and 1 invented logic issue. The core todo CRUD operations are well-implemented, but many auxiliary endpoints (sessions, profile, account, password) appear fabricated. Guest session controller uses wrong DTO types. Overall, the implementation shows good faithfulness for core features but poor relevancy due to many unsupported endpoints.

lines.text: * Query and filter active member sessions.
* Register a new member account with email and password credentials.
* Creates a new temporary guest identity for unauthenticated visitors.
* Create a new todo task for the authenticated member.
* Update the authenticated member's profile information.
* Permanently delete the authenticated member's account and all associated data.
* Retrieve a paginated list of edit history entries for a specific todo.

items.message: Empty type alias: IUpdate = {} — missing properties

lines.text: * Request body for updating member profile. Note: Currently no editable fields available as the database schema only contains authentication-required fields.
* Request body for refreshing member authentication tokens.
* Query and filter active member sessions.
* Register a new member account with email and password credentials.
* Creates a new temporary guest identity for unauthenticated visitors.
* Create a new todo task for the authenticated member.
* Update the authenticated member's profile information.
* Permanently delete the authenticated member's account and all associated data.

penaltyRecovery.description: Add JSDoc to 9 missing APIs (90%) to recover up to 4 points
